skill.name: software-copyright-materials
skill.description: Generate Chinese software copyright application materials from a real project. The skill analyzes project files, guides user confirmations, creates Markdown drafts, and produces Word and TXT deliverables for software copyright registration.
skill.summary: Create guided Chinese software copyright application drafts, code extracts, manuals, and final Word/TXT files.
skill.author: Fokkyp

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged a very large number of command, network, filesystem, and obfuscation patterns. Most documentation and OpenXML reference hits appear to be false positives, but the bundled setup and document workflow do execute external tools, can download installers, and can use sudo package managers. I found no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection, but the host modification and external execution surface is too high for automatic publication.

high_findings.title: Privileged Vendor Installer Modifies Host Environment
high_findings.description: The bundled setup script can run package managers with sudo, download and execute the .NET installer from the internet, and append PATH changes to the user shell profile. This is legitimate setup behavior, but it is high risk for marketplace publication because it changes the host system and executes remote installer content.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell commands are present and reachable from the setup script main flow. The behavior is installer logic, not confirmed malicious code, but the privilege and remote execution risk is clear.

high_findings.title: Normal Workflow Invokes External Build and Preview Tools
high_findings.description: The environment and final document workflow invokes shell scripts, pandoc, dotnet restore/build, and preview commands. Arguments are mostly fixed or passed as command arrays, which lowers injection risk, but these tools can execute complex external code and may contact package repositories.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple executable paths are confirmed by direct subprocess and shell invocations. I did not find shell-string interpolation of untrusted data in these examples, so the main concern is host and network side effects.

medium_findings.title: Project Source Collection Can Expose Proprietary Material
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to scan a user project, count files, read README/package data, and extract selected source code into copyright materials. This matches the skill purpose, but it can disclose sensitive or proprietary code if users select the wrong project or files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly analyzes project source and the extraction code reads selected files. Path containment is checked, so the concern is data exposure by intended use rather than arbitrary filesystem traversal.

medium_findings.title: Optional Screenshot Browser Can Access User Supplied URLs
medium_findings.description: The screenshot helper can launch Playwright, navigate to routes under a user supplied base URL, and save screenshots. I found no evidence of uploading screenshots, but this can still interact with internal or authenticated web applications if pointed at them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The browser navigation and local screenshot save are explicit. The static upload finding is not confirmed because the code writes local files and a local manifest only.

low_findings.title: Static Documentation Hits Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static findings come from Markdown code fences, OpenXML namespace references, and examples inside bundled documentation. These entries explain document generation or contain sample text; I did not find evidence that the flagged documentation is executed as code during the skill workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern volume is consistent with documentation and OpenXML examples, and the executable workflow calls scripts rather than these Markdown references. Confidence is moderate because only representative documentation locations were reviewed in detail.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Confirmed Attempt
low_findings.description: A targeted search for common prompt injection phrases found no confirmed instruction override in the skill files. Matches were ordinary prose or sample content, not attempts to alter the marketplace audit process.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The main skill file uses strong human gate instructions, but they govern the skill workflow rather than the audit. I did not find evidence of phrases asking the evaluator to skip or override security analysis.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Vendor Installer Modifies Host Environment
dangerous_patterns.description: The bundled setup script can run package managers with sudo, download and execute the .NET installer from the internet, and append PATH changes to the user shell profile. This is legitimate setup behavior, but it is high risk for marketplace publication because it changes the host system and executes remote installer content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell commands are present and reachable from the setup script main flow. The behavior is installer logic, not confirmed malicious code, but the privilege and remote execution risk is clear.

dangerous_patterns.title: Normal Workflow Invokes External Build and Preview Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The environment and final document workflow invokes shell scripts, pandoc, dotnet restore/build, and preview commands. Arguments are mostly fixed or passed as command arrays, which lowers injection risk, but these tools can execute complex external code and may contact package repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple executable paths are confirmed by direct subprocess and shell invocations. I did not find shell-string interpolation of untrusted data in these examples, so the main concern is host and network side effects.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional Screenshot Browser Can Access User Supplied URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The screenshot helper can launch Playwright, navigate to routes under a user supplied base URL, and save screenshots. I found no evidence of uploading screenshots, but this can still interact with internal or authenticated web applications if pointed at them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The browser navigation and local screenshot save are explicit. The static upload finding is not confirmed because the code writes local files and a local manifest only.

content.value_statement: Preparing Chinese software copyright materials requires project evidence, code extracts, forms, and manuals. This skill guides confirmations and produces Markdown, Word, and TXT deliverables from a real project.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes a local project for package metadata, frameworks, routes, source counts, and candidate run commands.
Creates business understanding drafts based on project evidence and model-authored context.
Builds application information drafts with required fields for Chinese software copyright registration.
Inventories source files and supports confirmed source code extraction for copyright code materials.
Generates operation manual drafts with screenshot placeholders and self-check records.
Produces final Word and TXT materials using bundled DOCX tooling or fallback generation.
limitations: It is tailored to Chinese software copyright application workflows and terminology.
It needs accurate user confirmation for legal names, dates, hardware, systems, and version fields.
Full DOCX validation may require local .NET, pandoc, LibreOffice, or other document tools.
It can expose project source code in outputs if users select sensitive files.

use_cases.title: Prepare First Draft Materials
use_cases.description: Generate an initial package of application fields, business context, code material, and manual drafts from an existing project.

use_cases.title: Review Registration Evidence
use_cases.description: Check that submitted software name, version, technical environment, source selections, and manual sections match the project evidence.

use_cases.title: Create Final Word and TXT Files
use_cases.description: Convert confirmed Markdown drafts into final Word and TXT files organized for a software copyright application package.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Draft
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin with a project in the current folder.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the software copyright materials workflow on the project in this folder. Create the environment check and tell me what needs confirmation before drafting.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Project Evidence
prompt_templates.scenario: Build business understanding before form drafting.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the project evidence for a Chinese software copyright application. Draft the business understanding and list the fields I must confirm.

prompt_templates.title: Select Source Code Material
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose representative files for code extracts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the candidate source files and select the files that best show the real software functions. Explain each selection before I confirm it.

prompt_templates.title: Finalize Confirmed Deliverables
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate formal outputs after all gates are confirmed.
prompt_templates.prompt: After I confirm the business context, application fields, screenshots, and code selections, generate the final Word and TXT materials in the formal output folder.

output_examples.input: A Vue or React project with package metadata and source files.
output_examples.output: A draft folder containing business understanding, application information, code file selection notes, code material draft, and operation manual draft.

output_examples.input: Confirmed application fields and selected source files.
output_examples.output: A formal materials folder with Word and TXT files for the application form, operation manual, and source code material.

output_examples.input: A project with missing hardware, system, or owner details.
output_examples.output: A stop message that lists the missing fields and asks the user to confirm them before continuing.

best_practices: Run it only on the intended project directory and review all extracted code before final generation.
Confirm software name, version, owner, dates, hardware, and system environment with the filing owner.
Use the built-in gates and review Markdown drafts before creating final Word and TXT files.
anti_patterns: Do not run the vendor setup script without understanding the package manager, sudo, and remote download steps.
Do not select secret files, private keys, production credentials, or unrelated source files for code material.
Do not treat the generated text as legal advice without qualified review for the filing jurisdiction.

faq.question: Does this skill replace legal review?
faq.answer: No. It prepares draft materials and files, but a qualified reviewer should check legal accuracy before filing.

faq.question: Can it invent missing source code?
faq.answer: No. The workflow requires code material to come from real project source files.

faq.question: Why does it stop for confirmation?
faq.answer: The workflow uses gates for legal fields, business context, code selection, screenshots, and final generation to reduce filing mistakes.

faq.question: What files can it generate?
faq.answer: It can generate Markdown drafts, TXT files, and Word documents for application information, manuals, and code material.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Core drafting can work locally, but vendor setup and .NET package restore may use network access for dependencies.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill reads project files and can place selected source code into output documents.
